answer:
No, these two traditions present fundamentally different views of pain (grief/sorrow in Iqbal, suffering/torment in Manu). Iqbal's "Falsafa-e-Gham" (*Philosophy of Sorrow*) portrays sorrow as an essential, transformative force in human life and spiritual growth—necessary for awakening the soul, polishing the heart, revealing life's secrets, and sustaining eternal love beyond death.[^1][^2] It is not punitive but a "silent song of soul" that enables flight, grace, and wholeness, without which life remains superficial and veiled.[^3]

In contrast, Manusmṛti Chapter 12 frames suffering (torments, hells, diseases, lowly rebirths, old age, painful deaths) explicitly as the retributive fruit of past *karma* (actions from mind, speech, and body), arising from sins and faults that propel souls through cycles of punishment and purification.[^4] Pain here is moral consequence—experienced in hells or vile wombs after death—graded by the *guṇas* (sattva, rajas, tamas) and expiated via penances, study, or endurance, ultimately leading toward higher states or liberation through Vedic action and self-knowledge.[^5]

Iqbal's Sufi-inspired Islamic lens elevates grief poetically as a divine polish and companion to love; Manu's Indic Dharmashastric view treats suffering mechanistically as karmic justice within a rebirth cycle. They overlap superficially in seeing pain as purposeful (spiritual refinement vs. purification), but diverge sharply: Iqbal rejects sorrow as mere affliction, while Manu ties it directly to ethical retribution.[^6]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 7 (*Falsafa-e-Gham*), [p-141d8d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-141d8d).
[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 7 (*Falsafa-e-Gham*), [p-e693fc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-e693fc).
[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch. 7 (*Falsafa-e-Gham*), [p-e436cc](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/07-falsafa-e-gham/translation/#p-e436cc).
[^4]: Manu, *Manusmṛti*, ch. 12, [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/).
[^5]: Manu, *Manusmṛti*, ch. 12 (e.g., Mn_12.3–12, 75–81 on karmic states/torments; Mn_12.82–107 on expiation/liberation), [full chapter](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/12/translation/).
[^6]: No direct cross-references exist in the corpus between these works on pain/grief.
